clean_story:
Two brain speeds. One split-second choice.

Thinking, Fast and Slow names the duel inside your head: System 1 (fast, automatic) vs. System 2 (slow, effortful). Like a camera’s autofocus (instant) versus manual mode (precise but clunky).

System 1 runs on shortcuts. See an angry face? It reacts before you "decide"—like slamming brakes at a shadow that might be a deer. It’s why "2+2" feels easier than "17×24" or why "BAT" reads faster than "SOFA" (your brain fills gaps from habit). But it trips: A bat and ball cost $1.10 total; the bat costs $1 more. Most blurt "$1.05 + $0.05" (wrong). System 1 leaps; System 2, lazy, nods along.

System 2 is the overseer—when it bothers. Solving "24×17" forces it awake, like a sleepy guard. Yet even then, it tires fast. Judges parole hearings? Approval rates drop from 65% to near 0% before lunch (hunger hijacks fairness). System 2’s willpower is a battery—drained by choices, from picking socks to resisting cake.

The trick? Spot when fast thinking fools you. A "90% fat-free" label feels healthier than "10% fat" (same math, different framing). Your brain isn’t broken—it’s optimized for speed, not truth.

Three related subjects are cognitive biases in ads, willpower as energy, and how chess masters think.
